cards.title: The Source Is The Stage You Don't Own
cards.teaser: Your pipelines start with someone else's data. The systems that produce it weren't designed for you, which makes them the most reliable source of unpleasant surprises.

runs.text: Source systems generate the data your pipelines depend on. They include:

items.text: — Postgres, MySQL, Oracle, MongoDB powering production applications.
— structured or unstructured streams of what apps are doing.
— Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub topics carrying business events.
— third-party services like Stripe, Salesforce, Zendesk.
— CSVs dropped on SFTP, exports from analytics tools, partner files.
— yes, still, frequently the source of truth for parts of the business.

runs.text: The unifying property: they exist for their own purposes, and you're a downstream consumer they didn't sign up to support. Their schemas evolve to serve their owning team's needs, not yours. Their availability and performance optimize for their primary workload, not yours. The data engineer's job at this stage is mostly about making peace with this asymmetry.
If you can list every source system feeding your pipelines and the team that owns each, you're already ahead of most data teams. Most can't.
Same as integrating with someone else's API — they didn't ship it for you, so they don't owe you stability.

cards.title: Schemas — Where Structure Lives
cards.teaser: Every source has a schema, even when it claims not to. The difference is whether the schema is enforced at write, at read, or by accident.

runs.text: Three philosophies for where schema enforcement lives:

items.text: — the storage system enforces structure when data is added. Postgres rejects rows missing required columns. Strict, predictable, friction at write time.
— data lands as-is; the consumer interprets structure when reading. JSON files in a lake; whoever queries them imposes shape. Flexible, fast to ingest, expensive to keep correct.
— no enforced structure at all. MongoDB documents, key-value blobs. Maximum flexibility, maximum operational risk.

runs.text: The choice isn't binary in practice. Modern lakehouses (Iceberg, Delta) blur the line — they enforce schema like a warehouse but on lake-format files. Streaming systems often combine schema registries (Confluent Schema Registry) with otherwise-loose payloads.

The data engineer's reality: you'll deal with all three. Operational source systems are usually schema-on-write. Lakes you control are often schema-on-read. Third-party APIs are wherever they happen to be.
"Schemaless" rarely means no schema. It means the schema lives in everyone's head and drifts silently. The bills come due downstream.
Same as type checking — strict at write, strict at read, or strict at runtime when something blows up. Pick where you want the cost.

cards.title: Schema Evolution Is Inevitable
cards.teaser: Source schemas change. Columns get added, renamed, deprecated. Pipelines that assume a fixed schema break in proportion to how much they assumed.

runs.text: Common schema-evolution events you'll face:

items.text: — usually safe; ignore it or pick it up.
— silent breakage; your code references the old name.
— values you parsed as int now arrive as string.
— your downstream queries return null where they used to return values.
— the column still exists with the same name and type, but means something different now (a status enum gets a new value, an integer changes its meaning).

runs.text: Defensive strategies:

items.text: Be in the loop on releases that touch source schemas.
Tools like Great Expectations, dbt tests, Monte Carlo can catch unexpected shape changes before consumers do.
for streaming data. Producers register schemas; consumers fetch them; incompatible changes fail at the registry, not in production.
when assumptions break, rather than silently producing wrong output.

runs.text: The hardest evolutions are semantic changes — the schema looks the same, the values are slightly different. Only the application team knows; only diligent communication catches it.
When a downstream consumer reports "the numbers look weird" and the pipeline is green, your first hypothesis should be a semantic change in the source.
Same as API versioning — backward compatibility is a contract you have to maintain on purpose, not a default state.

cards.title: Operational Databases As Sources
cards.teaser: Most source data still lives in production OLTP databases. Reading from them safely without hurting the application is a craft of its own.

runs.text: Production databases are the most common source. The constraints:

items.text: Long-running analytical queries can lock tables, exhaust connections, or evict working-set pages from cache.
Reading rows over many minutes risks seeing updates mid-stream.
Full-table scans on multi-billion-row tables don't fit normal HTTP timeouts.
The application team will refactor schema; your reads should not block their freedom to do so.

runs.text: Approaches:

items.text: Replicate the database; query the replica. Shifts the load off the primary but requires replica infrastructure.
Periodic snapshot of the primary; query the snapshot. Loses some freshness, fully isolates the production database.
Stream the database's transaction log; reconstruct state externally. Near-real-time and zero impact on the primary.
The application team exposes data through APIs you query. Most isolated; depends on the team prioritizing your needs.

runs.text: CDC has won as the modern default for high-volume operational sources. Tools like Debezium make it production-ready.
Querying production databases directly works fine until it doesn't. The first time your analytical query takes the application down, you'll wish you had a replica.
Same as scraping a website — you might get away with it for a while, until the rate limits or page structure or admin team catches you.

cards.title: Change Data Capture Reads The Database's Diary
cards.teaser: Instead of querying for new rows, CDC tails the database's transaction log to capture every change as it happens. Real-time, low-impact, and increasingly the default for operational sources.

runs.text: Every transactional database keeps a write-ahead log (WAL) of every change — inserts, updates, deletes — for crash recovery. CDC tools tail that log and emit each change as an event.
The wins:

items.text: Reading the log doesn't compete with application queries.
Updates and deletes show up too, not just new rows.
Transactions arrive in the order they committed.

runs.text: The mechanics:

items.text: — logical replication slots stream WAL records.

runs.text: Tools that productionize this: Debezium (open source, the de facto standard), Fivetran's CDC connectors, AWS DMS, Striim.
The challenges:

items.text: still require coordination — CDC streams what the schema is, not what you wished it were.
— when the WAL grows faster than you read it, you fall behind.
— these often produce tricky CDC events that downstream consumers handle poorly.
— CDC starts from a point in time; you need to backfill historical data separately.

runs.text: CDC has steadily replaced batch full-table refreshes as the default for ops-to-analytics pipelines. The freshness and impact wins are big enough to justify the operational complexity.
Same idea as following a git commit log instead of diffing the whole repo every hour. Cheaper, more current, requires more setup.

cards.title: APIs And Webhooks As Sources
cards.teaser: Third-party SaaS systems mostly expose data through APIs. Pulling efficiently while respecting rate limits is the daily craft of API-based ingestion.

runs.text: When the source is a third-party service (Stripe, Salesforce, Zendesk, Hubspot, GitHub), you're querying or receiving from APIs.
Pull patterns
— your code requests data on a schedule:

items.text: — re-fetch everything every run. Simple, expensive, often required if there's no incremental field.
— fetch records changed since last successful run. Requires the API to support a
— fetch records after a returned cursor token. Vendor-specific; each API does it differently.

runs.text: Push patterns
— the source sends data to you when it changes:

items.text: — the API POSTs to your endpoint when an event occurs. Fast, but you must run a reliable receiver, handle retries, and reconcile missed events.
— some vendors (Shopify, Stripe with Connect) offer stream subscriptions.

runs.text: Cross-cutting concerns:

items.text: — every API has them; respect them or get throttled or banned.
— OAuth tokens expire; bearer tokens rotate; build retry around 401s.
— most APIs return pages; assume you'll need to traverse them all.

runs.text: This is where managed connectors (Fivetran, Airbyte) earn their fee — they handle the API quirks for hundreds of sources so you don't.
If you're tempted to write a custom Salesforce connector, look at what the managed vendors charge first. The build cost is almost always worse than the recurring fee.
Same job as scraping a third-party service — politeness, reliability, and reconciliation are the unglamorous half of it.

cards.title: Files As Sources — Still Everywhere
cards.teaser: SFTP drops, S3 buckets full of CSVs, partner-shared exports — file-based sources are unfashionable but they're not going away.

runs.text: File sources remain common because they're the lowest common denominator. The patterns:

items.text: — a partner uploads a daily file to SFTP or S3.
— the source service pushes a file to your endpoint when ready.
— your job fetches files from a known source.

runs.text: The challenges file sources bring:

items.text: — CSVs with different delimiters, quotes, null handling between batches.
— half-uploaded files mistaken for complete ones; use checksums or markers (
— mid-month a vendor changes the filename pattern and your job breaks.
— UTF-8 vs Latin-1; BOMs; mixed encodings within a single file.
— a file that was 100MB last month is 5GB this month.

runs.text: What helps:

items.text: — every file lands in a predictable location with a predictable naming pattern.
— schema, row count, format checks before any further processing.
— bad files move to a quarantine directory so they don't block the pipeline.
— if the same file arrives twice, your pipeline handles it correctly.

runs.text: "It's just a CSV" is the phrase that precedes 80% of file-ingestion incidents. CSV is one of the worst data formats ever; assume it will misbehave.
Same as parsing user-uploaded files — assume malice or incompetence in the source; validate early.

cards.title: Streaming Sources And Event Logs
cards.teaser: Many systems now publish events as they happen. Consuming streaming sources is operationally different from polling — and increasingly the default for high-velocity data.

runs.text: Streaming sources publish events continuously to a message bus (Kafka, Kinesis, Pub/Sub, Pulsar). Examples:
What's different about consuming them:

items.text: You run a long-lived consumer process, not a scheduled job.
You track which event you last read; restart from there.
When you can't keep up, the broker holds events; you must catch up before retention expires.
vary — per partition usually, global rarely.
— possible in some setups, hard in general; aim for at-least-once with idempotent processing.

runs.text: What this enables:

items.text: Real-time analytics, dashboards that update within seconds.
Reactive pipelines — downstream actions fire as events arrive.
Decoupling — producers don't know who consumes.

runs.text: The cost: streaming infrastructure (Kafka, Flink, schema registries, monitoring) is more complex than batch. Reach for streaming when the freshness requirement justifies the operational burden.
Many teams default to streaming and regret it. If your downstream consumer reads daily, your source can be batch. Streaming when you don't need it is expensive complexity.
Same trade-off as polling vs WebSocket — push is more current, more complex; pull is simpler, less fresh.

cards.title: Source Quality — Garbage In, Garbage Out
cards.teaser: Every quality issue downstream traces back to either the source data or a transformation bug. Sources are usually the first place to look — and the hardest place to fix.

runs.text: Categories of source-data quality issues:

items.text: — fields that are null when they shouldn't be.
— the same event recorded multiple times.
— names with weird characters, dates in mixed formats, mixed timezones.
— values that should have updated but haven't.
— ages of 800, negative quantities, future dates of birth.
meaning either "complete" or "abandoned" depending on context.

runs.text: Approaches:

items.text: — actually look at the data; understand its quirks.
— Great Expectations, dbt tests, Monte Carlo run quality checks on every pipeline run.
— the cheapest fix is at the source; data engineers spend disproportionate time advocating for upstream changes.

runs.text: You won't fix all of it. The realistic goal is knowing about quality issues before downstream consumers do, and making informed decisions about what to fix vs what to document and live with.
When you discover a source-data issue, the question isn't only "how do I clean it?" — it's also "should this be fixed at the source so other consumers don't suffer the same problem?"
Same as input validation in software — fix at the boundary if you can, defend internally where you can't.

cards.title: Schemas Need Owners, Not Just Definitions
cards.teaser: A schema everyone uses but no one owns is a schema that drifts silently. Source systems and the tables they feed need explicit human ownership for quality to hold.

runs.text: Ownership in data systems means:

items.text: for the source's correctness, freshness, and stability.
when the source breaks downstream consumers.
when the schema can change and how to communicate it.

runs.text: Without ownership, sources accrete entropy. Columns get added with names like
temp_field_2
. Tables get joined to other tables in ways no one remembers why. Definitions drift as new use cases bolt on.
Mature data orgs assign explicit owners — usually a team, sometimes an individual — to each significant source. The owner:

items.text: Communicates breaking changes to consumers ahead of time.

runs.text: Where the ownership model gets interesting is at the boundary between application teams (who own the source database) and data teams (who own the pipelines). The data mesh push has been about making domain teams own data products, not just operational databases.
"Everyone owns it" means no one owns it. If you can't name the owner of a source within 30 seconds, the source has no owner.
Same as code ownership — without a CODEOWNERS-equivalent, every change becomes someone else's problem.

cards.title: Working With Application Teams
cards.teaser: The application engineers who own your sources are your most important relationship. Treating them like a service desk wastes the relationship; treating them like collaborators makes the pipelines more reliable.

runs.text: The data team often has a fraught relationship with application teams. Common patterns of friction:

items.text: — application team ships a release that breaks pipelines.
— application team blames the data team for read load on their database.
— application team renames or repurposes a column without telling anyone.
— application team doesn't know which pipelines depend on what.

runs.text: What helps:

items.text: Show up before you need something.
Build the relationship in normal time, not during incidents.
Document which application team owns which sources. Share the list with them.
A schema-change checklist, an automated test in their CI that flags breaking changes — these reduce surprise and don't require their bandwidth ongoing.
"Your DB is slow because of our queries; here's a CDC pipeline that fixes it" goes further than complaint.
They have product deadlines too; data team needs are usually not their top priority.

runs.text: The teams with the strongest data quality and the fewest incidents are not the teams with the best tools — they're the teams whose data engineers and application engineers know each other's names.
A monthly informal sync between the data team and the largest source-owning teams pays for itself in incidents avoided. Doesn't have to be formal; needs to be regular.
Same as cross-team API contracts — works because of the relationship, not the document.
